definition: Property of many NoSQL systems not to enforce a rigid database schema on write.
Schema is interpreted only when data is read by application or query logic.
Database chosen specifically for one application and its access patterns.
Shared database for several applications with strong focus on data integration.
Externally, the system behaves as if there was only one sequentially processed copy.
Every request to a non-failing node must receive a response.
The system continues operating despite a network partition.
The network splits into groups that temporarily cannot exchange messages.
Time span until replicas become consistent again after an update.
Metadata used to compare versions of a data item in distributed systems.
Conflicts are allowed and resolved later by application-specific logic.
Conflicts are prevented upfront by locking or coordination.
Writes of the same session are observed in that order by others.
A write based on a read preserves that causal dependency.
Query that looks up exactly one value by its key.
Storage layout where changes are appended sequentially instead of overwritten.
Currently writable Bitcask file to which new entries are appended.
Closed data file that is no longer modified.
Insert or update operation for a key-value pair.
Read operation returning the current value of a key.
Delete operation that typically writes a tombstone in log-structured stores.
Bitcask process that cleans old data files and keeps only live newest values.
Active sorted in-memory structure receiving new LSM writes.
Full memtable that is no longer written and is being flushed to disk.
First SSTable level of an LSM-tree where key ranges may overlap.
Index storing only block boundaries or fence pointers rather than every key.
Positive test result although the searched element is actually absent.
Negative test result although the searched element is actually present.
Query over a key interval rather than exactly one key.
Additional physical storage usage relative to the logically live data.
Text-based document format made of objects, arrays and simple values.
MongoDB maximum document size of 16 MB.
Storing an ID in one document that points to another document.
List of IDs in a document to reference several other documents.
1:n relationship with few dependent objects, often suitable for embedding.
1:n relationship with many but bounded references, often suitable for reference arrays.
1:n relationship with very many dependent objects, usually better modelled as a separate collection.
Replica-set node that accepts MongoDB writes.
Replica-set node that replicates the primary's operations.
MongoDB server storing metadata and configuration of a sharded cluster.
Rules with which MongoDB can check document structure and fields of a collection.
Schema format describing allowed fields and types of a JSON document.
Node of a property graph, typically representing an entity.
Directed connection between two nodes in a property graph.
Name or type of a graph relationship, such as ACTED_IN or HAS_AIRPORT.
Key-value attribute on a node or relationship.
Sequence of nodes and relationships in a graph.
Cypher path with a variable number of relationships, such as *1..3.
Path of length 0 containing only the start node.
Cypher clause for finding graph patterns.
Cypher subclause that constrains a MATCH/OPTIONAL MATCH pattern or filters intermediate rows after WITH.
Cypher clause for outputting expressions, variables or aggregates.
Cypher keyword for removing duplicates.
Cypher aggregate function collecting values into a list.
Cypher aggregate counting result rows.
Constraint enforcing unique property values for a label.
Constraint requiring a property to exist.
Neo4j mechanism for respecting causally dependent transactions in later reads.
Storage layout that stores complete tuples or rows together.
Splitting a relation into column-wise fragments.
Stored tuple identifier next to every column value.
Storage reduction through a more compact representation of column values.
Index structure using one bit vector over row positions per value.
Combining separate column values into complete tuples or intermediate results.
List of position IDs marking qualifying rows in a column.
Operator fetching values of a column at given positions.
Operator swapping pairs from (position,value) to (value,position).
Operator keeping only the left component or position from pairs.
Large read-optimized main storage area of a column store.
Small update-friendly storage area for recent changes.
Bigtable row key by which data is sorted lexicographically.
Fine-grained column name inside a Bigtable column family.
Version component of a Bigtable cell.
Google's highly available distributed lock service supporting Bigtable coordination.
